content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Jumanji (1995)

**Score:** 87
**Body:** did anyone else think this was quite a scary film as a kid? I can't quite put my finger on it but it definitely gave unsettling vibes, it had a sinister undertone throughout I always thought. 

the music used in it was creepy also & just the whole make up in general, lots of scenes that just felt "off" for whatever reason.

it's ironic, how the "film cover" makes it look like a light hearted family comedy with the picture of Robin grinning (RIP)

amazing film though 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I got weirded out after watching it again and noticing that the actor that plays Alan's dad is the same actor that plays Van Pelt the game hunter.


They pulled a Captain Hook on us
  - Omg I just commented this!!! I had no idea. My friend was like yea everyone knows that
2. It almost feels lovecraftian in that, there’s this other dimension called Jumanji that we don’t know enough about. All we have are these killer animals and a hunter coming from there who doesn’t even seem human. It’s fear of the unknown.  

Like The Mist for kids.
3. > I can't quite put my finger on it but it definitely gave unsettling vibes, it had a sinister undertone throughout I always thought.

Doesn't the kid get sucked into the game world and goes missing in the real world and is assumed dead?
  - Not just assumed dead, rumored to have been chopped up and hidden in the walls of the house by his own father...
  - yes
4. The fact that Van Pelt is literally hunting a human being is very unsettling.
  - The Most Dangerous Prey
5. I was just talking about this and that I had nightmares from it.  The murderous, barbed flowers and the ginormous mosquitoes really freaked me out.
  - ... don't forget the "run... it's a stampede" bit 😭 the fear

## 2. Go see «Train Dreams»!

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Caught Train Dreams kind of randomly and it completely surprised me. It is slow and calm in the best way, never dull, and the atmosphere is incredible. The visuals and sound make you feel like you are really there, and the performances feel natural and real. I have been thinking about it ever since I left the theater. If you enjoy quiet, reflective movies that let things breathe, I definitely recommend it. This is one of those films that reminds me why going to the cinema is worth it.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Slow, steady, and achingly human.
2. I completely agree hoping for an Oscar nomination for Joel Edgerton really great performance
3. I watched it a few days ago for the first time and it's in my personal top ten greatest films of all time now.
4. Excellent movie. I saw it on one of the streaming platforms. I wish it was screened in cinemas where I live. 

Highly recommended.
5. Beautiful film
6. It took me a minute to realize it was the brother from Warrior
7. Totally agree. Cinematic masterpiece.
8. I liked it a lot, very touching, made me think for a long time after
9. It was absolutely lovely

## 3. Any movies similar to kubrick's "EYES WIDE SHUT" masterpiece?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Kubrick's Eyes wide shut is one of my fav films of his.....mystery blended with peak of erotism...never seen something like that.

I saw the movie 5 years ago...never thought about it much,but when I rewatched it recently I was actually mind blown for its erotism through dialogues in the first half and complete mystery in second half ..thats some crazy idea.

Forget to mention CRASH by cronenberg also my fav erotism blended thriller(but not too mystery)

Do comment if you know any erotism blended mystery movie.by the way I also liked THE VOYUERS..it was kinda similar bit.


### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mulholland drive by David lynch
2. Hmmmmm. 



- under the silver lake 

- don’t look now 

- night moves

- memento

- the consequences of love (no sex really, but lots of sexual tension and it’s a great mystery and has an amazing sound track)

- diaboliique
  - I am big fan of under the silver lake....it has less erotic scenes but mystery blended fantasy is way far more than any movie i have seen.those decoding.
3. Love Eyes Wide Shut, too. Actually just saw it again at the cinema last night.

Have you seen Picnic at Hanging Rock? O Lucky Man (less mystery in that one, but certainly erotic and surreal)? Nic Roeg’s Bad Timing?
  - Never seen these three....which one should I see first.
4. Blue Velvet (1986), but honestly sounds like you’d love David Lynch in general
5. After Hours is very similar just from the perspective of a single guy trying to get laid instead of a married doctor.
6. Maybe Don't Look Now. Or Birth.
7. I'm gonna suggest Blow-Up but it's probably low on the erotic angle. It is voyeuristic thought and has a certain erotic quality.
8. I wonder if Ang Lee's Lust, Caution would satisfy this wish of yours.  It gets a very high eroticism score, the plot has to do with espionage -- woman spy take assignment to seduce Tony Leung.  So it's got eroticism plus strong high-tension plot -- I no longer remember details but I do remember how successfully erotic I thought it was, maybe including a "too much" quality which normally might put me off, but in this one it didn't -- and also beautiful.

Maybe other people will weigh in on this one.

## 4. Edge of seat survival thrillers

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I need edge of my seat survival thrillers like train to busan, 47 meters down, cast away, etc where you are sitting there super anxious for them going “omg make it to the next room” “don’t get caught” “dont get eaten” “don’t turn on that light” stuff like that. Nail biting suspense hoping they make it. Please include year of movie! Thanks!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Revenant (2015)
2. The Edge
3. Apocalypto 

Cliffhanger
  - Apocalypto should be considered a classic man
4. Ready or Not is great.
  - Yeah man, second this. Anytime i have a bad day this is part of my final girl trilogy.

Ready or Not (2019, Sam Weaving)

The Hunt (2020, Betty Gilpin)

No One Will Save You (2023, Kaitlyn Dever)
5. 127 Hours
6. Fall (2022)
7. Sorcerer (1977)
8. "The Grey" (2011)

"Lights Out" (2017)

"Don't Breathe" (2016)

"Hush" (2016)

"Dredd" (2012)

"Cube" (1997)

"Assault on Precinct 13" (1976)

"The Warriors" (1979) Original Theatrical Version

"The Texas Chainsaw Massacre" (1974)

"Predator" (1987)

"A Nightmare on Elm Street" (1984)

"Crank" (2006)

"Crank 2" (2009)

"First Blood" (1982)

"Escape Room" (2019)"

"Escape Room 2: Tournament of Champions" (2021)

"The Running Man" (1987)

## 5. movies that are similar in theme and/or essence of The Sweet East, Red Rocket, or The Starling Girl, etc.

**Score:** 6
**Body:** i am craving an off-beat fliratious dramedy and i don't know how to explain this vibe.  

  
other movies include  like palo alto and priscilla. damn thinking about all these movies maybe the genre is jsut chronically online as fuck 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think you’d like the show: “The Death of Bunny Munro”

And in case you haven’t seen it, “Boogie Nights” (1997)
  - for starts i ADOREEE boogie nights so hell yeah cheers && omfg that show looks SO up my alley! crazy bc i call my friends "bunny" annnddd Nick Cave wrote that and his son is in The Sweet East... good omen! thank u for the recs! <3
  - Haven't heard of that Bunny Munro show but Boogie Nights is perfect for this vibe, good call

  
You might also dig Spring Breakers or The Florida Project if you want that same weird energy but different settings
2. Buffalo ‘66 and Smooth Talk might be right up your alley.
  - oo i love buffalo 66 ill have to check out smooth talk thank uuuuu <3
3. Breaking The Waves (1996)
4. An Education (sortof)


The Dreamers 


The Virgin Suicides 


Vincent N Roxxy (not a great movie but the scenes with the two leads were the best part/watchable - but didn't finish it since rest of the plot was mid.)


American Honey  - maybe the most like those films. 
5. Pedophile's wet dream movies you mean?
6. licorice pizza, anora

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Honest Antagonists

**Score:** 1275
**Body:** The first time I rewatched Sicario I noticed that Matt Graver is basically honest the whole movie, except for the bit that CIA can only operate in the US with a local agency attached which comes up in the end.  The whole movie when asked what they are doing he says they are trying to “make enough noise” to bring out the boss which they do.  

There is something about a villainous character who is honest about they are doing that makes for an interesting character.  

I guess if I had a question it would be what to you makes a compelling villain and why? Also can you think of any villains that were pretty honest and forthright or maybe even right about somethings? 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His character was so good here
  - Mature josh Brolin is gold i must say.  
Everything he's been doing for a while is great.
2. Lord Farquaad kept his deal with Shrek
3. Does the dude from 10 Cloverfield Lane count? (John Goodman)
4. The Rock - Brigadier General Francis X. Hummel
5. In Heat, DeNiro is a straight shooter with everyone except Amy Brenneman’s character. 

Pacino: stop taking scores

DeNiro: no, also i will kill you if you try to get in my way
- - - 
DeNiro: this is my last heist, but i do not think you should do it. i would walk away if i were you. 

Sizemore: for me 🙂‍↕️😏 the action is the juice
  - Except himself. 

He doesn’t believe his own bullshit. He can’t drop everything and go. He HAD to go kill Waingro.  
    - Mf could drop everything he loved but not everything he hated
6. Man of Steel: general Zod. He is honest in the entire movie and even the hero of his own story with good intentions. He is even trying to reason with Kal El, multiple times and ways - from position of power, treating him as an equal, and even begging him.

They do not understand each other because they are different on a genetically coded level (which is a HUGE part of the movie and understanding the failure of Krypton and it's people, and the whole backstory), but Zod is genuinely the good guy from his own perspective. Its not malice, hatred, or power that motivates him but to save his own civilization, culture, people. Can you imagine the hundreds of thiusands of ants who's forts you might have destroyed, when the foundations of your house was built? Did you even think about the morality of that?

Humans are the ants for Zod. And not because he chooses to believe that, but because he is programmed to prioritise Krypton and its people above all else. He couldnt change if he wanted to, he wasn't taught to be like that, he was DNA level coded. And so, from his own perspective, "Every action I take, no matter how violent, how cruel, is for the greater good of my people. And now... I have no people. My soul. That is what you took from me!" He says so, at the end. Only when the last hope of saving his people is lost, is when he loses his shit at the very end. And I know he has to go, I know he has to die, or otherwise he'll never stop killing. But try to be in his shoes, the man witnessed the death of his world, spent 30 years traveling across the galaxy trying to find a still living colony, and when he finally found a sign of life and he rushed to the help signal, he is faced with a man with the key to restoring Krypton and he says no, for...living with ants. And from your point of view, he is completely unreasonable, no matter how hard you try, and when as a last resort you attempt it violently, he destroys everything you needed create life again. Because despite the threatening looks, Zod and his crew arrived with machines that were used not to destroy, but to create life - the world engine and the genesis chamber. All he needed was the designed DNA database of all kryptonians... flowing in Kal El's blood. All he needed was a drop of blood, and a world that can be terraformed into Krypton. Unfortunately, the latter was Earth.
7. Javier Bardem No Country for Old Men  
Hugo Weaving The Matrix

## 2. Klaus (2019) is one of the best Christmas movies of all time

**Score:** 1003
**Body:** On Netflix

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I wish Criterion would release the physical release.
2. Watched it for the first time last night and love it.
  - Same. It was great.
3. Hands down the best Christmas origin story ever. Super underrated IMO.
4. Agreed. It’s one of our Christmas favorites. It’s funny and heartfelt. Love this movie
5. Love this movie. I keep trying to get my family to watch it but they wont because "iTs AniMaTEd"
  - That’s wild. Some of the best movies out there are animated.

Howls Moving Castle

Ghost in the Shell

Toy Story

Triplets of Belleville

Book of Kells

The Jungle Book
6. Agreed, it even has Norm Macdonald
7. Hell yes! Gets me emotional every time
8. Saw it tonight for the 1st time and it was wonderful.

## 3. Die Hard is still the greatest action movie ever made.

**Score:** 161
**Body:** It's ability to captivate, to survive the times, to offer a plot complex enough to rival more "respected" filmmaking.

The performances, the constant turnover of power and control dynamics, the occasional infusion of character growth, and then the way it all comes together for the finale. It's just so well done. Films today can only dream of being this well-crafted.

It's not just the greatest action film ever made, it's one of the greatest films ever made. Period.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Aliens posting up
  - They mostly post at night, mostly.
    - They should have nuked it from orbit, it was the only way to be sure.
2. There are many contenders, everyone is entitled to their opinion. Die Hard is definitely up there no question.
3. Predator (1987) is the perfect action movie. Aliens (1986) is a close second. Terminator 2 (1991) takes the third place.
  - i honestly think True Lies belongs on this list
    - What kind of sick bitch takes the ice cube trays?
4. Terminator 2 is better
5. Maybe im in a minority here but for me it will always be the first matrix movie.
6. Predator, Aliens, T2 and Die Hard are all fighting for top and amazing action films

## 4. Home Alone might be the most rewatchable Christmas movie ever

**Score:** 97
**Body:** No matter how many times it’s on, I still stop and watch.
What Christmas movie does that for you??

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are three we watch every Christmas and I still enjoy, Home Alone, Christmas Vacation, and The Muppets Christmas Carol. My kids (3 & 7) giggled their way through the muppets for the first time this year, and it was a genuinely lovely moment.
  - Your kids will now be well rounded human beings, well done
2. A Christmas Story

Bad Santa

Elf

Home Alone

Home Alone 2: Lost in New York

National Lampoons Christmas Vacation
3. The Holiday. I’ve seen it a ridiculous number of times and it still works every single time. It’s cozy, low stakes, and I don’t even have to fully pay attention to feel good.
  - Agreed, and I still want to punch the cheating boyfriend every time he's on screen !
4. Trading Places


Christmas Lampoons 


Planes, Trains, and Automobiles


Home Alone 1/2


Santa Claus: The Movie
5. Its a Wonderful Life. I've memorized it. And A Christmas Story.
6. Only Christmas Lampoon's and Scrooged with Bill Murray
7. I’d agree if Gremlins didn’t exist

## 5. I hate to admit it but this is one of my comfort movies. It’s hilarious when it really wasn’t meant to be 😂

**Score:** 42
**Body:** The acting, the dialogue, the story. It’s all just too hilarious for words to describe. Why is everyone in this movie acting so weird? It feels like little kids wrote and starred in this. But I gotta admit, this is one of my comfort movies. I’m watching it right now and I can’t help but laugh every time someone says anything 😂

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I kind of think all of Shyamalan’s movies have a weird charm to them.  Even if they’re “bad”
  - Now that you mention it, I think you’re right lol
  - Even the last airbender?....
2. Whhhaaaattt? Noooooo!
3. The epic plot twist in this is that Mark Wahlberg didn't know he was filming a movie
4. I wish I could watch this for the first time again, definitely a head scratcher. I was in disbelief a lot of the time, such a strange movie, and hilarious too.
5. Absolutely, it's the shitty everything about it that makes it good.
  - It’s hilarious that 25% into the movie there is this crazy idea that it might be the wind. It’s hilarious that it turns out to be the wind! 😂

Classic Shamalan: have a great opening to a film, and give away the “plottwist ending” before we’re even halfway. In The Village it is even faster than this. 😁
6. One of the worst ever made. ‘Evil wind’.
7. I don't know why you're dissing on a movie you liked. The only thing that makes a good movie is that it is entertaining and as far as I'm concern this movie was. We need a Happening 2!

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. A question about Terence Malick's later films

**Score:** 36
**Body:** I am slowly getting into the work of Terence Malick. Having already seen Badlands and Days of Heaven, I plan to watch The Thin Red Line in the coming weeks. I am aware that Malick's films become increasingly less narrative-driven  after Tree of Life. I understand some people find his films after this period to be plotless, pointless, self indulgent and pretentious. For those who are fans of these films, I am curious to learn more about what you enjoyed about them. What did you get out of these films? Do you have to go into these films with a certain mindset in order to appreciate them? Do these films contain the depth of his earlier work in a more subtle way? Thanks

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There is a triptych of films he made in the 2010s – To the Wonder, Knight of Cups, and Song to Song – which are rather meandering and often disliked by even his biggest fans. At least as a significant fan of his work, these three films did not work for me but I think that any person could find something to like in them depending on their tastes. I think they all have excellent music in them. But among his later work also includes A Hidden Life. Which has not been discussed yet here either. Yet I find it to be among his best films and one of the best films of the last decade. Please seek out A Hidden Life. I think it makes a great, though heavy, double feature with Jonathan Glazer's Zone of Interest.
2. His films, especially his later films (Tree of Life, New World) are visual poems. He's concentrating on lyricism and movement and flow to discuss themes more than traditional narrative structures. I have found his later films almost universally breathtaking and I find myself reflecting on my own life and my own priorities (especially as I get old as shit) after watching them.  Even if you don't like them now, they may resonate later.
  - Very well said

The pictures (especially the new world) are gorgeous - it may sound corny, but it is like watching a painting on the screen

The thin red line is a meditation on life - as visceral as saving private ryan is, I prefer TTRL as a movie

The tree of Life is a completely unique movie watching experience, also a meditation on life
3. His later films became more about capturing an emotion and he went all in on “show, not tell” to the point where his characters barely exposit anything if they say anything at all. If he wanted to capture the feeling of adolescence in Tree of Life then he shows a lot of scenes of kids playing with their mom who nurtures them, and then their angry father who scolds them.

His later films are more “moving paintings” that capture a message and/or mood than they are traditional narrative-driven films with a story and a plot.
4. The Tree of Life is the closest thing to a Kubrick film in the 21st century. Douglas Trumbull, who worked on 2001, also worked on The Tree of Life.

I would argue the themes of Tree of Life are even more compelling and broader in scope than those of 2001. Certainly more humanistic.

But geez why ask about his films before watching them? Just watch them, draw your own conclusions, then come here for discussion. I don’t want to give away anything on a film you haven’t seen yet. Just watch it.
  - I get very little Kubrick from Malick. The two movies are maybe equally ambitious, but their approaches are so different. Kubrick never seemed to seek the transcendent in humanity, he wanted to strip us bare and show us for what we are. Maybe more accurately I don’t see Kubrick seeking a redemptive transcendence the way Malick does. The 2001 ending is transcendent but it is very alien and motivated by external forces rather than like a spiritual culmination.
  - I feel that Jonathon Glazer's Birth is the closest thing to a Kubrick film in the 21st century.
  - What does "closest thing to a Kubrick film mean". If anything, that's a descriptor that limits The Tree of Life. 
5. They will feel self indulgent at times and your attention span will meander while watching but his films are like dreams that linger in your mind after you wake up. I still remember the light from the tree of life and the sense of in-betweenness I felt when I watched the film.

Edit: when I look at a classic piece of abstract art I am not necessarily looking to interpret the art literally (eg, look for symbols, etc). Instead I am trying to explore what feelings the art arouses in me. That is how I interpret Malick films - you interrogate the themes by exploring the emotions the film arouses in you. It makes experiencing his films feel deeply personal. I appreciate his ambition as a filmmaker. 

If the only emotion his films arouse in you is boredom that’s fair but maybe you’re also not putting in effort as a viewer either. It’s not pretentious to try to open yourself up to art and grow through the experience.
6. Tree of Life is my favourite of his, I found it to be hauntingly beautiful and like visual poetry into other peoples memories. To enjoy it though I think you have to be in the mood for that type of experience.  

It’s like music where some things just emotionally vibe with you, and if they don’t it might come off as pretentious or uninteresting.

## 2. Kubrick's intention behind EWS

**Score:** 24
**Body:** The essence of the film is revealed by the line "the end of the rainbow." I am sure this has been discussed before, but the movie, apart from being a pretty faithful adaptation of a novel, is a critique of how materialistic society has become. The movie is set during Christmas, but the only indication of this are presents, parties, and ornaments. The only ritual is one which endorses unrestraint rather than piety. Bill fantasizes that if he pursues "the end of the rainbow," and attains the luxuries and acceptance of the members at the party, then his insecurities, including those towards Alice will evaporate. Every person in the movie basically serves some transactional purpose, some more overt like the costume shop owner's daughter, others less conspicuous like Alice. The real horror of the film is that society is not made up of people, it's made up of commodities. There are so many details that reveal Bill's enslavement by materialism, from Bill's apartment, to the toy store he walks around in the final scene.

The confession at the start of the film exposes Bill's materialistic worldview, that money can buy anything, including Alice's thoughts, which sets him on a path to find "the end of the rainbow" to redeem himself. No matter what status, wealth, or supposed power the people at the party can offer him, they ultimately live empty existences, which validates the trope that money doesn't buy happiness or love for that matter. The partygoers engage in insatiable vampiric vices. The masks they wear are their true faces, self-hating, insecure, and consumed by worldly possession. At the end of the film, Bill's eyes are wide open to the real "end of the rainbow" which is his love for Alice, whether she is waiting for him there or not, and with that he confronts his insecurity and awakens from the nightmare. The original novel doesn't suggest this much, but Kubrick always had his own spin when making adaptations. Kubrick held a disdainful nostalgia for America, New York City, and especially Manhattan. It's a love story after all.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I always thought the core of the movie was the wife's speech and how the high moral of the protagonist was based on ignorante of the full reality.
He proceeds to unveil the truth during the movie and is presented with a choice: dive into this underworld or remain as he was. He chose the latter, but now by making a real choice.
It is a bit like Blue Velvet.
  - I've never considered the parellels with Blue Velvet before, but that's fascinating. You're right.
2. Good analysis. Christmas is also about anticipation without fulfillment. It’s a big empty promise. Just about everyone William encounters reacts to him sexually (even the group of young guys who gleefully call him a f*ggot), but there’s no sex for him. This is a movie about not getting laid. 

And people complain that Kubrick’s New York doesn’t look real, but it’s supposed to look fake. 

No original thoughts here, I read a lot about this movie when it came out and ideas stuck with me.
  - Do you remember all the online gossip in early forums during its production? It was such an exciting time. I was 17, and every lunch time at college I'd go to the college library to read the latest comments. I remember the early photos released, one of Bill holding the newspaper, and it was just a wild time anticipating the film. Its strange to think that back then, Traumnovelle wasn't so easily accessible, so people didn't really have a clue what it would be about.
    - The best review I read back then that I’ll post at some point looks at how the marketing did the movie a disservice because it teased an erotic NC-17 vibe between Hollywood’s top superstar couple. The whole slogan was basically 
CRUISE | KIDMAN.
  - Yeah to me the main point of EWS is it's a comedy of errors about an emasculated man being given every opportunity to have the sex outside his marriage he's so desperately seeking and somehow fumbling the bag every time. It's a bit similar to Barry Lyndon in that I think the prestige and aesthetics of Kubrick (Barry Lyndon also has added challenge of needing to work against people seeing a stuffy period piece as well) cause people to miss the dry humour. Of course there are deeper themes about relationships and consumerism and exploitation as everyone talks about but I do also think that Kubrick made a fair few films that just make the funniest choice regardless of any thematic implications and sometimes that applies to EWS for me where a lot of people are trying to read some hidden meaning (which is not to pull an, "It's not that deep bro" because I hate that kind of artistic interpretation, but I do think in this case sometimes people miss the forest for the trees and forget that this film is really funny).
    - His take on Lolita brought out the darkly satirical aspects of the novel. 
3. Even though the movie changes a lot of the stuff from the source material it actually does a great job at communicating the central theme of male insecurity and the need for self-assertion. Pretty much every narrative element serves this purpose and in the second half he gets the reality check on how pathetic and clueless his own schtick is. And then in the very end his wife delivers a haymaker to seal the deal.
4. There is ample scholarship on *Eyes Wide Shut* and it is worth reading most of it before attempting to assert one’s own reading of the film. In this case, it is well documented that Kubrick was interested in Schnitzler’s novel all the way back to the early 1960s, when it was still too early to be nostalgic about America and NYC, and arguably too early for his wish to adapt it being based on money and materialism.
5. I watched this movie for the third time this year and it always reveals new things to me, which I love. I definitely agree with your assessment of materialism, especially as you see Bill fork out cash constantly on the spot to get what he wants. 

But I also think the film is abut toxic masculinity and patriarchy. Bills "dream" where he is searching for sex involves ridiculous unrealistic displays of women throwing themselves at him out of nowhere. It's definitely a fantasy where the women are not actual human beings, but visions of pleasure to stroke his ego. He has a hot wife at home and seemingly their sex life is still great. Yet he's still not fulfilled, he still needs more because she acted not like an object or possession with no feelings, but like an autonomous woman struggling with monogamy. 

The sex cult dehumanizes women to the point that they don't even have a face, and they all seem to have the exact same body type. He will never be happy or fulfilled by getting what he supposedly wants, because he sees women as objects that can satisfy his ego, and these objects are out of reach because it's a secret "club" of elite men and he's not in it. He's using women as social currency.

I guess along with the patriarchy/materialism I also see it being about humans constantly striving for what they don't have, at any cost. Human greed is pervasive and entirely connected to our ego and views on how we are perceived. Alice feels bored and without purpose as a stay at home wife, and has dreams of throwing it all away. But this again connects to the power structure of patriarchy and toxic masculinity, because she's under the thumb of her rich husband and all of the status and illusion that comes with that. 

I love this film so much!

## 3. Did The Way of Water’s HFR criticism affect how Fire and Ash is being handled?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** 3Y ago a user posted,

https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/s/Km99OoNvdW

So, One of the biggest criticisms of Avatar: The Way of Water wasn’t the story or visuals, but the high frame rate implementation, especially the switching between 48fps and “fake” 24fps (frame-doubled). A lot of viewers felt the changes were noticeable and distracting, pulling attention away from the film rather than enhancing it.

Many people weren’t against experimentation itself — the concern was that it felt less like an intentional artistic decision and more like a technical workaround, something that made the movie feel closer to a game, VR experience, or tech demo than a dreamlike cinematic space.

This also raised a broader worry about the future of cinema:
if variable frame rates become standard, do we risk losing the illusion and emotional softness that 24fps has traditionally provided?

With Fire and Ash,
Did Cameron stick to the same HFR approach, or has there been any adjustment based on the reaction to The Way of Water?
And for those who’ve followed production closely — do you think the criticism influenced how the Fire & Ash is presented?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >With Fire and Ash, Did Cameron stick to the same HFR approach, or has there been any adjustment based on the reaction to The Way of Water?


It seems pretty much the same. It's maybe a little better, but I don't think there was any change in approach. It's still just Cameron watching the movie down and flagging which shots he wants at which frame rate. 
  - And the flagging seems random

I really wish he'd just do everything in HFR
    - Yeah, it’s really jarring. A human-scale scene will switch to HFR a few shots ahead of going into action, and it’s like a video game cutscene switching out of pre-rendered graphics to gameplay. Actually, I think there are much more random cases than that of flipping back and forth shot to shot.

I don’t know how this feels for people who don’t game, but it just makes me feel like the GPU is chugging. It always felt bad to crank down to 24 and always felt good to get back to 48. This is all based on the 3D experience, where I think the benefits of 48 are even more pronounced.
2. I absolutely hated HFR in The Hobbit, found it a bit distracting in Avatar 2, and didn’t notice it at all in Avatar 3.  

I really feel for those of you who can perceive the shifting between frame rates so acutely - that sounds annoying as hell. I’m curious if you have been trained on high frame rate in a way I haven’t. I’m a bit older and spent almost no time with fast-refresh gaming, for example. I presume everyone else on reddit is a younger-than-me gamer, so… could that be a factor?  

Edit: I dug a big deeper since I was curious about the possibility that you could "train" your perception of frame rate. In academic terms, this is referred to as things like temporal resolution / temporal order perception / simultaneity. Below I'll just say POFR (perception of frame rate) as a shorthand for brevity. Here are a few things I learned:  
• Gamers tend to have more acute POFR ([ref](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S105381192300054X?via%3Dihub)), but that could be correlation... not causation.  
• A study showed that POFR could be "trained" via doing a computer-based task ([ref](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00221-007-1254-z)); this suggests that gamers could be actively improving their POFR via gaming. Good for headshots - bad for enjoying a movie with fluctuating frame rate.  
• Several studies found that POFR tends to decline naturally as you age ([ref](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2774211/)). So maybe me aging 3 years since the last Avatar movie is making me tolerant of the frame rate switching!
  - I noticed it constantly in *Way of Water* and I'm a Gen X gamer who has no problem with a gaming frame rate of 30 fps and doesn't see the need for a monitor that goes about 60.

The HFR made the film almost totally unwatchable. One of the ugliest films I've ever seen.
3. I'm unsure, but there were definitely some scenes in Fire and Ash that made me feel like I was watching a video game cut scene rather than actual actors. 

I don't know whether I'm praising video games here or insulting the movie, to be honest, as it wasn't bad. It just didn't feel like watching a movie at times.
4. I hate the switching frame rates. I notice the switch every time, its like playing a Vsynced Game at 48Hz with the frame rates constantly switching between 24 and 48 because the system cant keep it at 48. It distracted me a lot in TWOW.
  - Same. It looks sped up or lagging for a few seconds every single switch
    - The switching especially is very grating. Just keep it at 48 if you really want the higher frame rate, but stop changing it all the time.
5. I actually think, like VR and videogames, the HFR works much better with 3D and this type of film. I'd rather the entire film be 48fps in 3D rather than switching back and forth constantly.

## 4. 1980 German New Wave Subgenre

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I'm interested in some discussion with regards to German films produced in and around the 1980s that have a certain stylish, nihilistic aesthetic. With an almost "rock star" affectation. Films you might consider “New Wave horror movies”. Films like Eckhart Schmidt’s *Der Fan* \[1982\] and to a lesser extent *Loft* \[1985\], Carl Schenkel’s *Strike Back* \[1981\] and *Out of Order* \[1985\]. Synth scores, but not exclusively. *Angst* \[1983\] is a little bit like this although it’s so fucking bleak and gruesome and it’s Austrian but that part doesn't even matter. *Supermarket* \[1974\] counts.  In the UK a guy named Barney Broom made a short film called *Knights Electric* \[1980\] that fits the aesthetic pretty well, too. Probably *No Mercy No Future* \[1981\] or *Knife in the Head* \[1979\] but I haven’t seen those yet. 

Does this type of cinema have a name or general classification to identify films of that ilk?  We have German Expressionism and Kammerspiel. Like “Grindhouse Fassbinder” but surely smarter. Or is it just considered part of New German Cinema overall?



### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's kinda interesting to think about the movies you mentioned (I've only watched some of them) as being part of a subgenre. Some other movies that could fit into this provisional  subgenre are Decoder (1984), The Gold of Love (a.k.a. Der Fan 2) (1983), White Star (1983), Verlierer (1987).

But maybe there's similar movies from other countries and from about the same-ish time, too, so this would not necessarily be a German subgenre but more a sign of the times. Like maybe Tetsuo (1989), Videodrome (1983) or Star Time (1992), each of these ticks one or more boxes. These are just off the top of my head and maybe a good fit or not, but maybe other people can come up with more fitting examples.
  - Great additions. Thanks! I don't know *any* of those films in the first paragraph. I know all the films in the second one, though. And while I definitely appreciate your position that its maybe not  exclusively a German subgenre, Tetsuo and Videodrome have their place at the "body horror" table and I'd leave them there. I think Star Time is closer to on-the-money.

You know, now that you mention it, there is a certain cross-pollination between these films we're discussing and some of the Ozploitation films of the time. *Celia* \[1989\]. *Crosstalk* \[1982\], NExt of Kin \[1982\]. Maybe even *The Quiet Earth* \[1985\]. *Razorback* \[1984\]? But that's just hypersylized monster movie stuff, I suppose. The more I think about this I find I'm beginning to simply describe "1980s low budget thriller" which is veering away from my original intent. I'm gonna stop here.

Thanks for the reply.

  - there will be blood is obviously not entirely a comedy, but i do think there are bits and scene that are meant to read as funny.

as dark and unsettling as those last two scenes are meant to be, i think they’re also meant to be a very over-the-top satire of the archetype of robber baron industrialist/fraudulent preacher that plainview and eli are meant to be. you’re supposed to be afraid of plainview because he’s a monstrous sociopath, but i also think you’re supposed to laugh at how ridiculous of a person he’s become over something as simple as oil.
    - I saw it in theaters and was like "Man, that was dark."
